From a design point of view this is unclean. `task_instance` is an instantiation of a `task` in time. As such it is bound to a `try_number`, which is now also provides.

I suggest to change the signature of the method to ` def read(self, task_instance, try_number=None)` and the return the latest log if `try_number` isn't specified or maybe even better _all logs_.

Or, use a different signature all together `def read(self, task, execution_date, try_number)`.

On a side note: the try_number thing still feels a bit off. Properly keeping track of _`start_dates`_ (i.e. recording each start_date somewhere) seems more logical. `try_number` in the end is the count of `start_dates`.

I think it would be better, since we have to think of how it affects users - if we define an `events` method on the base, that means no HasTraits can ever define its own `events` method or attribute. We should be very conservative about what names we are reserving on a base class, and try to make them trait-specific where we can. While `trait_events` may seem redundant on HasTraits, it isn't on `InteractiveShell` or other HasTraits descendants, where it really matters.

Originally, in the second comment for this PR, I followed the approach from [here](https://github.com/cloudfind/google-pubsub-asyncio/blob/master/app.py). Testing it locally made me realize that it doesn't _actually_ work (if you run with `PYTHONASYNCIODEBUG=1`, there's a lot of issues going on. This commit mainly fixes that approach, with some other minor things that I noticed were wrong (i.e. inverted some args passed into google libs) as well as touched up some things (`pubsub_msg._ack_id` is actually a long string like `'projects/a-project/subscriptions/test-sub:1413'` and not a number, which isn't nice for logs and such).

While google's pubsub package allows for message polling, [it actually blocks](http://google-cloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pubsub/subscriber/index.html#subscription-callbacks) (aka the rest of the entire gordon service would be blocked). Therefore, I chose to use a [threadpool executor](https://github.com/spotify/gordon-gcp/pull/9/commits/5299d682957d7d575e2f3f2a4b849067f99613e2#diff-5d2065d110f9259f5221a7aec9ef5ff6R435) to unblock. 

The polling functionality requires a callback function (to process a message), but that too is also blocking (only non-async funcs accepted). So garnering heavy inspiration from this [stack overflow post](https://stackoverflow.com/a/29302684), I elected to implement a small wrapper around the `threading.Thread` class with its own event loop (separate from the Main event loop). If a task from another thread is handed to it – which it is, from the main event loop – it runs it in its event loop with explicit threadsafety handling (via `loop.call_soon_threadsafe`). If it's handed a task from its own thread, then it just runs it on its own loop directly (no threadsafety handling needed).

What I suggested is relying in your environment variables by default, so until developers hard code settings, that works like you're saying and it should respect 12-Factor App configuration (so `os.environ` is used without writing anything in your Django settings).

In that way if you put the Django app in a container without writing APM settings, and you pass env variables, it will work as you're expecting. On the other hand, if you hard code the values because you want to programmatically force something, env variables will be ignored. That case is not 12-Factor compliant but users may expect that if you hard-code something, it will be used with highest priority. So with my suggestion, just don't hard-code anything and it would pick your env variables.

This particular use case is at first glance a bit odd.  One of the main points of PSF photometry is to use the *shape* information to get a better constraint on the flux and centering. So it would be odd to want to independently measure each sigma on its own.  The "sky background" use case is much more practical, but that's a bit more complicated to implement because there's not a natural model to use.

So maybe something like this:  
```
... to fit more model parameters than just the flux and centering.  
While a more realistic use case might be fitting sky backgrounds, 
or shape parameters of galaxies, here we use the ``sigma`` 
parameter in `IntegratedGaussianPRF` as the simplest possible
example of this feature.
```

Alternatively, you could adapt the example to use the astropy `Gaussian2D`, and have x_stddev/y_stddev/theta *all* be free parameters.  That would then sort of be like galaxy-fitting photometry. But that might get a bit overly-complex, so whichever you think is going to be clearer is fine, @mirca
